texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Communication cost and privacy are two major considerations in federated learning (FL). For communication cost, gradient compression by sketching the clients' transmitted model updates is often used for reducing per-round communication. For privacy, the Gaussian mechanism (GM), which consists of clipping updates and adding Gaussian noise, is commonly used to guarantee client-level differential privacy. Existing literature on private FL analyzes privacy of sketching and GM in an isolated manner, illustrating that sketching provides privacy determined by the sketching dimension and that GM has to supply any additional desired privacy. In this paper, we introduce the Sketched Gaussian Mechanism (SGM), which directly combines sketching and the Gaussian mechanism for privacy. Using RényiDP tools, we present a joint analysis of SGM's overall privacy guarantee, which is significantly more flexible and sharper compared to isolated analysis of sketching and GM privacy. In particular, we prove that the privacy level of SGM for a fixed noise magnitude is proportional to 1 / √ b , where b is the sketching dimension, indicating that (for moderate b ) SGM can provide much stronger privacy guarantees than the original GM under the same noise budget. We demonstrate the application of SGM to FL with either gradient descent or adaptive server optimizers, and establish theoretical results on optimization convergence, which exhibits only a logarithmic dependence on the number of parameters d . Experimental results confirm that at the same privacy level, SGM based FL is at least competitive with non-sketching private FL variants and outperforms them in some settings. Moreover, using adaptive optimization at the server improves empirical performance while maintaining the privacy guarantees.
texts.text: Communication cost and privacy are two major considerations in federated learning (FL). For communication cost, gradient compression by sketching the clients' transmitted model updates is often used for reducing per-round communication. For privacy, the Gaussian mechanism (GM), which consists of clipping updates and adding Gaussian noise, is commonly used to guarantee client-level differential privacy. Existing literature on private FL analyzes privacy of sketching and GM in an isolated manner, illustrating that sketching provides privacy determined by the sketching dimension and that GM has to supply any additional desired privacy. In this paper, we introduce the Sketched Gaussian Mechanism (SGM), which directly combines sketching and the Gaussian mechanism for privacy. Using RényiDP tools, we present a joint analysis of SGM's overall privacy guarantee, which is significantly more flexible and sharper compared to isolated analysis of sketching and GM privacy. In particular, we prove that the privacy level of SGM for a fixed noise magnitude is proportional to 1 / √ b , where b is the sketching dimension, indicating that (for moderate b ) SGM can provide much stronger privacy guarantees than the original GM under the same noise budget. We demonstrate the application of SGM to FL with either gradient descent or adaptive server optimizers, and establish theoretical results on optimization convergence, which exhibits only a logarithmic dependence on the number of parameters d . Experimental results confirm that at the same privacy level, SGM based FL is at least competitive with non-sketching private FL variants and outperforms them in some settings. Moreover, using adaptive optimization at the server improves empirical performance while maintaining the privacy guarantees.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Federated learning (FL) [44] is a widely used machine learning framework where a shared global model is trained under the coordination of a central server using data distributed across various clients. In FL, each client carries out training steps on its local data and sends only the model updates back to the server, which then refines the global model by suitably aggregating these local updates. Because the data remains on the clients, FL can offer enhanced privacy protection compared to traditional centralized learning. Nonetheless, FL faces two significant challenges: (1) it lacks a rigorous privacy guarantee (e.g., differential privacy (DP)) [17] and has been shown to be susceptible to various
texts.text: Federated learning (FL) [44] is a widely used machine learning framework where a shared global model is trained under the coordination of a central server using data distributed across various clients. In FL, each client carries out training steps on its local data and sends only the model updates back to the server, which then refines the global model by suitably aggregating these local updates. Because the data remains on the clients, FL can offer enhanced privacy protection compared to traditional centralized learning. Nonetheless, FL faces two significant challenges: (1) it lacks a rigorous privacy guarantee (e.g., differential privacy (DP)) [17] and has been shown to be susceptible to various

texts.text: inference attacks, leading to local information leakage during training [49, 50, 75, 83, 84]; and (2) it usually requires a high communication overhead due to the frequent communication between the server and the client [35]. Many recent advances in FL have been motivated by these two challenges.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Towards reducing communication costs, a key goal has been to reduce the communication cost per round by compressing local updates from clients to a lower dimension [30, 36], including sparsification [40, 70, 5], quantization [4, 41, 52], and sketching [59, 54, 28]. Among these approaches, sketching methods stand out for its simplicity, making it easier to integrate with existing FL methods. As an unbiased compressor, it does not require bias correction using error feedback mechanisms, unlike sparsification, which incurs additional memory costs [57, 60]. As a linear operation, sketching ensures the geometric properties are approximately preserved after compression [13] as opposed to quantization distorting the inner product structure, potentially slowing convergence [4].
texts.text: Towards reducing communication costs, a key goal has been to reduce the communication cost per round by compressing local updates from clients to a lower dimension [30, 36], including sparsification [40, 70, 5], quantization [4, 41, 52], and sketching [59, 54, 28]. Among these approaches, sketching methods stand out for its simplicity, making it easier to integrate with existing FL methods. As an unbiased compressor, it does not require bias correction using error feedback mechanisms, unlike sparsification, which incurs additional memory costs [57, 60]. As a linear operation, sketching ensures the geometric properties are approximately preserved after compression [13] as opposed to quantization distorting the inner product structure, potentially slowing convergence [4].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Towards preserving privacy, two DP definitions are commonly considered in FL algorithm design: sample-level privacy and client-level privacy, where client-level privacy is the stricter guarantee in the sense that it ensures the output remains statistically indistinguishable when an entire client's dataset is altered, rather than merely hiding the inclusion or exclusion of individual data samples as sample-level privacy [81]. Various FL algorithms [21, 71, 63, 66] have focused on client-level privacy by adapting standard differential privacy techniques from centralized training (i.e., clipping gradients and adding Gaussian noise to the clipped values [1, 19]) to the federated learning setting.
texts.text: Towards preserving privacy, two DP definitions are commonly considered in FL algorithm design: sample-level privacy and client-level privacy, where client-level privacy is the stricter guarantee in the sense that it ensures the output remains statistically indistinguishable when an entire client's dataset is altered, rather than merely hiding the inclusion or exclusion of individual data samples as sample-level privacy [81]. Various FL algorithms [21, 71, 63, 66] have focused on client-level privacy by adapting standard differential privacy techniques from centralized training (i.e., clipping gradients and adding Gaussian noise to the clipped values [1, 19]) to the federated learning setting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Though privacy and communication-efficiency have mostly been studied independently, there have been some efforts towards solving these two challenges together. D 2 P-FED[71] combines stochastic quantization and random rotation [44] techniques to lower communication costs, to the discrete Gaussian mechanism for privacy [9], along with secure aggregation [7] to mitigate noise magnitude. Alternatively, DPSFL [80] directly applies the standard Gaussian mechanism to the communicationefficient federated learning algorithm FetchSGD [54]. However, these methods merely select individual compression and privacy components and stitch them together, treating communication-efficiency and differential privacy as separate concerns rather than examining their intrinsic interplay.
texts.text: Though privacy and communication-efficiency have mostly been studied independently, there have been some efforts towards solving these two challenges together. D 2 P-FED[71] combines stochastic quantization and random rotation [44] techniques to lower communication costs, to the discrete Gaussian mechanism for privacy [9], along with secure aggregation [7] to mitigate noise magnitude. Alternatively, DPSFL [80] directly applies the standard Gaussian mechanism to the communicationefficient federated learning algorithm FetchSGD [54]. However, these methods merely select individual compression and privacy components and stitch them together, treating communication-efficiency and differential privacy as separate concerns rather than examining their intrinsic interplay.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: [37] is one of the very few works to investigate the underlying relationship between differential privacy and communication efficiency in distributed learning. It demonstrates that the Count Sketch algorithm [10], despite being originally developed for communication efficiency, inherently satisfies a form of differential privacy for distributed learning algorithms. However, their results have several major limitations. First, when the privacy level ϵ provided by the Count Sketch mechanism falls short of the requirement, additional Laplacian or Gaussian noise must be injected, but there is no precise characterization of the amount of noise required. Second, their theoretical guarantees require arguably impractical assumptions, including the input (client gradients) to follow a Gaussian distribution and the sketching dimension b ≲ √ d where d is the input (gradient) dimension, which contradicts typical empirical configurations in sketched distributed learning, where b is often a fixed fraction of d , e.g., b = d/ 100 , to avoid derailing the optimization.
texts.text: [37] is one of the very few works to investigate the underlying relationship between differential privacy and communication efficiency in distributed learning. It demonstrates that the Count Sketch algorithm [10], despite being originally developed for communication efficiency, inherently satisfies a form of differential privacy for distributed learning algorithms. However, their results have several major limitations. First, when the privacy level ϵ provided by the Count Sketch mechanism falls short of the requirement, additional Laplacian or Gaussian noise must be injected, but there is no precise characterization of the amount of noise required. Second, their theoretical guarantees require arguably impractical assumptions, including the input (client gradients) to follow a Gaussian distribution and the sketching dimension b ≲ √ d where d is the input (gradient) dimension, which contradicts typical empirical configurations in sketched distributed learning, where b is often a fixed fraction of d , e.g., b = d/ 100 , to avoid derailing the optimization.

texts.orig: In this paper, we introduce the Sketched Gaussian Mechanism (SGM), which combines an isometric Gaussian sketching transform [59] with the classical Gaussian mechanism. Leveraging tools from Rényi differential privacy (RDP) [46] with the subsampling, post-processing, and composition theorems of differential privacy [18], we derive a tight upper bound on the overall privacy level ϵ of SGM. Concretely, with all other hyperparameters held fixed, ϵ = O ( 1 √ bσ 2 ) , where b denotes
texts.text: In this paper, we introduce the Sketched Gaussian Mechanism (SGM), which combines an isometric Gaussian sketching transform [59] with the classical Gaussian mechanism. Leveraging tools from Rényi differential privacy (RDP) [46] with the subsampling, post-processing, and composition theorems of differential privacy [18], we derive a tight upper bound on the overall privacy level ϵ of SGM. Concretely, with all other hyperparameters held fixed, ϵ = O ( 1 √ bσ 2 ) , where b denotes

texts.orig: the sketch dimension and σ 2 g the variance of the added Gaussian noise. Our new result for SGM establishes a clear dependence of ϵ on both b and σ 2 g . Unlike prior work, our result imposes no restrictive upper limit on b , thereby covering practical regimes in which b is a fixed fraction of the ambient dimension d . More importantly, this bound implies that for suitably large b , SGM achieves strictly stronger privacy guarantees than the standard Gaussian mechanism, demonstrating that the sketching operation itself confers inherent privacy benefits.

Unlike prior work, our result imposes no restrictive upper limit on b , thereby covering practical regimes in which b is a fixed fraction of the ambient dimension d . More importantly, this bound implies that for suitably large b , SGM achieves strictly stronger privacy guarantees than the standard Gaussian mechanism, demonstrating that the sketching operation itself confers inherent privacy benefits.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We further integrate SGM into a federated learning (FL) framework, referred to as Fed-SGM, supporting flexible choices of server optimizers to match practical deployment needs. We prove that this Fed-SGM satisfies client-level privacy guarantees. Moreover, by fully leveraging the fastdecaying spectrum of the deep-learning loss Hessian [77, 82, 76], which implies a small absolute intrinsic dimension [26], we establish rigorous optimization convergence bounds that scale only logarithmically in the ambient dimension d and linearly in this absolute intrinsic dimension, ensuring scalability to high-dimensional problems.
texts.text: We further integrate SGM into a federated learning (FL) framework, referred to as Fed-SGM, supporting flexible choices of server optimizers to match practical deployment needs. We prove that this Fed-SGM satisfies client-level privacy guarantees. Moreover, by fully leveraging the fastdecaying spectrum of the deep-learning loss Hessian [77, 82, 76], which implies a small absolute intrinsic dimension [26], we establish rigorous optimization convergence bounds that scale only logarithmically in the ambient dimension d and linearly in this absolute intrinsic dimension, ensuring scalability to high-dimensional problems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We empirically validate our approach on deep learning models for both vision and language benchmarks. Across these tasks, federated SGM requires strictly less Gaussian noises than the standard DP-FedAvg algorithm [81] to achieve the same privacy guarantee, and consistently delivers comparable or even superior model accuracy. Furthermore, when we replace vanilla gradient descent on the server with an adaptive optimizer, we observe additional accuracy gains, highlighting the practical benefit of incorporating adaptive optimization into our privacy-preserving federated learning framework.
texts.text: We empirically validate our approach on deep learning models for both vision and language benchmarks. Across these tasks, federated SGM requires strictly less Gaussian noises than the standard DP-FedAvg algorithm [81] to achieve the same privacy guarantee, and consistently delivers comparable or even superior model accuracy. Furthermore, when we replace vanilla gradient descent on the server with an adaptive optimizer, we observe additional accuracy gains, highlighting the practical benefit of incorporating adaptive optimization into our privacy-preserving federated learning framework.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 provides the description of our SGM and establishes the privacy guarantee. Section 3 introduces Fed-SGM, the application of SGM into federated learning setting, and establish optimization analysis of our framework. In Section 4, we present experimental evaluations that assess performances of our Fed-SGM and compare them with existing approaches. Finally, Section 5 concludes our contributions and highlight potential future directions. Due to space limit, we present our discussion on related works in Appendix A.
texts.text: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 provides the description of our SGM and establishes the privacy guarantee. Section 3 introduces Fed-SGM, the application of SGM into federated learning setting, and establish optimization analysis of our framework. In Section 4, we present experimental evaluations that assess performances of our Fed-SGM and compare them with existing approaches. Finally, Section 5 concludes our contributions and highlight potential future directions. Due to space limit, we present our discussion on related works in Appendix A.

texts.orig: Definition 2.1 (Sketched Gaussian Mechanism (SGM)) . For any statistic θ ( D ) ∈ R d of the dataset D , the Sketched Gaussian Mechanism outputs SG ( θ ; R,ξ ) = Rθ + ξ , in which R ∈ R b × d is a Gaussian sketching matrix with each entry sampled i.i.d. from N (0 , 1 √ b ) and ξ ∈ R b follows the Gaussian distribution N ( 0 , σ 2 g I b ) .
texts.text: Definition 2.1 (Sketched Gaussian Mechanism (SGM)) . At each step of training, we compute the gradient for a random subset of examples, apply clipping and SGM to each gradient, and update the parameter with the optimizer OPT using the aggregated gradient.

texts.orig: 2.1 Warm Up: Privacy Analysis by Moments Accountant
texts.text: 2.1 Warm Up: Privacy Analysis by Moments Accountant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Compared to the standard Gaussian mechanism [18], SGM incorporates an additional sketching operation. By the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma [13], sketching can be interpreted as a distancepreserving embedding that approximates the original high-dimensional update in a lower-dimensional subspace. Under this viewpoint of sketching, we obtain the privacy guarantee for Algorithm 1 by an extension of the moments accountant analysis of Gaussian mechanism [1]. We state the main theorem below with the full proof in Appendix C.1.
texts.text: Compared to the standard Gaussian mechanism [18], SGM incorporates an additional sketching operation. By the Johnson-Lindenstrauss lemma [13], sketching can be interpreted as a distancepreserving embedding that approximates the original high-dimensional update in a lower-dimensional subspace. Under this viewpoint of sketching, we obtain the privacy guarantee for Algorithm 1 by an extension of the moments accountant analysis of Gaussian mechanism [1]. We state the main theorem below with the full proof in Appendix C.1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 2.3. Theorem 2.2 shows that, holding all other parameters fixed, the privacy level ϵ p is monotonically decreasing with the sketching dimension b . This can appear counterintuitive at first, so we outline the intuition. According to the previous calculation, SG ( γ t ( D ); R t , ξ t ) ∼ N ( 0 , ( ∥ γ t ( D ) ∥ 2 b + mσ 2 g ) I b ) . As b grows, the signal term ∥ γ t ( D ) ∥ 2 b vanishes and the noise term mσ 2 g dominates, making the output distribution increasingly independent of D . Consequently, for any two neighboring datasets D and D ′ , the distributions SG ( γ t ( D ); R t , ξ t ) and SG ( γ t ( D ′ ); R t , ξ t ) become increasingly similar, since both are essentially noise-driven. Lemma 2.2 formalizes this: for a fixed noise multiplier σ g /τ , the α -Rényi divergence between these two distributions is bounded by α 2 τ 4 ( α -1) bσ 4 g , so that larger b strictly decreases the divergence. Therefore, increasing b makes it harder to distinguish which dataset produces the sketch, which implies a higher privacy level.
texts.text: Remark 2.3. Theorem 2.2 shows that, holding all other parameters fixed, the privacy level ϵ p is monotonically decreasing with the sketching dimension b . This can appear counterintuitive at first, so we outline the intuition. According to the previous calculation, SG ( γ t ( D ); R t , ξ t ) ∼ N ( 0 , ( ∥ γ t ( D ) ∥ 2 b + mσ 2 g ) I b ) . As b grows, the signal term ∥ γ t ( D ) ∥ 2 b vanishes and the noise term mσ 2 g dominates, making the output distribution increasingly independent of D . Consequently, for any two neighboring datasets D and D ′ , the distributions SG ( γ t ( D ); R t , ξ t ) and SG ( γ t ( D ′ ); R t , ξ t ) become increasingly similar, since both are essentially noise-driven. Lemma 2.2 formalizes this: for a fixed noise multiplier σ g /τ , the α -Rényi divergence between these two distributions is bounded by α 2 τ 4 ( α -1) bσ 4 g , so that larger b strictly decreases the divergence. Therefore, increasing b makes it harder to distinguish which dataset produces the sketch, which implies a higher privacy level.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 2.4. For the original Gaussian mechanism, i.e., G ( γ t ( D )) = γ t ( D ) + ξ ′ t where ξ ′ t ∼ N ( 0 , σ 2 g I d ) , G ( γ t ( D )) and G ( γ t ( D ′ )) are Gaussian distributions with the same variance σ 2 g , but different means γ t ( D ) and γ t ( D ′ ) respectively. So their Rényi divergence is α 2 ∥ γ t ( D ) -γ t ( D ′ ) ∥ 2 2 σ 2 ≤ α 2 τ 2 2 σ 2 , which is fixed for any dimensions. In contrast, for the SGM, SG ( γ t ( D )) and SG ( γ t ( D ′ )) are two Gaussians with the same mean of 0 but different variances ∥ γ t ( D ) ∥ 2 2 b + mσ 2 g and ∥ γ t ( D ′ ) ∥ 2 2 b + mσ 2 g , and their Rényi divergence is proportional to O ( 1 b ) according to Lemma 2.2. This offers an intuitive justification for the additional 1 / √ b factor in Theorem 2.2's privacy bound, as compared to the privacy guarantee of the standard Gaussian mechanism [1].

So their Rényi divergence is α 2 ∥ γ t ( D ) -γ t ( D ′ ) ∥ 2 2 σ 2 ≤ α 2 τ 2 2 σ 2 , which is fixed for any dimensions. In contrast, for the SGM, SG ( γ t ( D )) and SG ( γ t ( D ′ )) are two Gaussians with the same mean of 0 but different variances ∥ γ t ( D ) ∥ 2 2 b + mσ 2 g and ∥ γ t ( D ′ ) ∥ 2 2 b + mσ 2 g , and their Rényi divergence is proportional to O ( 1 b ) according to Lemma 2.2. This offers an intuitive justification for the additional 1 / √ b factor in Theorem 2.2's privacy bound, as compared to the privacy guarantee of the standard Gaussian mechanism [1].

texts.label: code
texts.orig: Algorithm 2 Fed-SGM Hyperparameters: server learning rate η global, local learning rate η local, noise scale σ g , clipping threshold τ , number of rounds T . Inputs: local datasets D c = { ( x i,c , y i,c ) } n c i =1 and loss function L c ( θ ) = 1 n c ∑ n c i =1 ℓ ( θ, ( x i,c , y i,c ) ) for clients c ∈ [ C ] . Initialize θ 0 randomly. for t = 0 , . . . , T -1 do Take a subset C t of N clients with sampling probability q = N C On Client Nodes: for c ∈ C t do Assign local initialization: θ c,t, 0 ← θ t Local update: for k = 1 , . . . , K do θ c,t,k ← θ c,t,k -1 -η local · g c,t,k end for Compute update: ∆ c,t ← θ t -θ c,t,K Clip the update: ˆ ∆ c,t = clip ( ∆ c,t η local , τ ) Apply SGM: ˜ ∆ c,t = η local SG ( ˆ ∆ c,t ( x i ); R t , z c,t ) Send ˜ ∆ c,t to the server end for On Server Node: Aggregate: ¯ ˜ ∆ t = 1 N ∑ c ∈C t ˜ ∆ c,t Broadcast ¯ ˜ ∆ t to the clients On Client Nodes: Update parameter: θ t +1 ← GLOBAL_OPT ( θ t , R ⊤ t ¯ ˜ ∆ t , η global ) end for Outputs: final model θ T

Initialize θ 0 randomly. for t = 0 , . . . , T -1 do Take a subset C t of N clients with sampling probability q = N C On Client Nodes: for c ∈ C t do Assign local initialization: θ c,t, 0 ← θ t Local update: for k = 1 , . . . , K do θ c,t,k ← θ c,t,k -1 -η local · g c,t,k end for Compute update: ∆ c,t ← θ t -θ c,t,K Clip the update: ˆ ∆ c,t = clip ( ∆ c,t η local , τ ) Apply SGM: ˜ ∆ c,t = η local SG ( ˆ ∆ c,t ( x i ); R t , z c,t ) Send ˜ ∆ c,t to the server end for On Server Node: Aggregate: ¯ ˜ ∆ t = 1 N ∑ c ∈C t ˜ ∆ c,t Broadcast ¯ ˜ ∆ t to the clients On Client Nodes: Update parameter: θ t +1 ← GLOBAL_OPT ( θ t , R ⊤ t ¯ ˜ ∆ t , η global ) end for Outputs: final model θ T

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Application in FL: Fed-SGM
texts.text: 3 Application in FL: Fed-SGM

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we introduce Fed-SGM, the application of the SGM within a federated learning framework that simultaneously achieves communication efficiency and differential privacy guarantees. We then establish its client-level privacy bounds and derive optimization convergence results. We consider a federated learning setting with C clients indexed by c ∈ [ C ] . Each client c holds a local dataset D c = { ( x i,c , y i,c ) } n c i =1 of size n c and defines its empirical loss by L c ( θ ) = 1 n c ∑ n c i =1 ℓ ( θ ; ( x i,c , y i,c ) ) , where θ ∈ R d is the model parameter and ℓ the per-example loss. The goal of the algorithm is to minimize the average empirical loss over clients, i.e., L ( θ ) := 1 ∑ C L c ( θ ) .
texts.text: In this section, we introduce Fed-SGM, the application of the SGM within a federated learning framework that simultaneously achieves communication efficiency and differential privacy guarantees. We then establish its client-level privacy bounds and derive optimization convergence results. We consider a federated learning setting with C clients indexed by c ∈ [ C ] . Each client c holds a local dataset D c = { ( x i,c , y i,c ) } n c i =1 of size n c and defines its empirical loss by L c ( θ ) = 1 n c ∑ n c i =1 ℓ ( θ ; ( x i,c , y i,c ) ) , where θ ∈ R d is the model parameter and ℓ the per-example loss. The goal of the algorithm is to minimize the average empirical loss over clients, i.e., L ( θ ) := 1 ∑ C L c ( θ ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Upon receiving client sketches, the server computes their aggregate and broadcasts this aggregated sketch back to every client. Each client inverts the sketch to recover the aggregated update in the original ambient space, and then updates the global model parameters in a single step via the GLOBAL_OPT operator. In this paper, we study the case with gradient descent (GD) and AMSGrad as GLOBAL_OPT .
texts.text: Upon receiving client sketches, the server computes their aggregate and broadcasts this aggregated sketch back to every client. Each client inverts the sketch to recover the aggregated update in the original ambient space, and then updates the global model parameters in a single step via the GLOBAL_OPT operator. In this paper, we study the case with gradient descent (GD) and AMSGrad as GLOBAL_OPT .

texts.orig: Remark 3.1. In the parameter-update step, we lift the aggregated sketch back to the ambient space using the transpose R ⊤ t , rather than the least-squares estimator R † t , i.e., the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse. This preference is motivated by the following points. First, since each entry of the sketching matrix R t ∈ R b × d is drawn i.i.d. Gaussian with mean 0 and variance 1 √ b , so E [ R ⊤ t R t ] = I d .

In the parameter-update step, we lift the aggregated sketch back to the ambient space using the transpose R ⊤ t , rather than the least-squares estimator R † t , i.e., the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse. This preference is motivated by the following points. First, since each entry of the sketching matrix R t ∈ R b × d is drawn i.i.d. Gaussian with mean 0 and variance 1 √ b , so E [ R ⊤ t R t ] = I d .

texts.orig: Therefore, for any vector g , E [ R ⊤ t R t g ] = g , which shows that R ⊤ t can recover g in expectation,
texts.text: Therefore, for any vector g , E [ R ⊤ t R t g ] = g , which shows that R ⊤ t can recover g in expectation,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: implying R ⊤ t is a near-optimal desketching operator for our use. Second, computing R † t each round requires solving a least-squares system, typically via QR/SVD, with per-round cost on the order of O ( b 2 d ) plus the overhead of forming and inverting a Gram matrix. In contrast, the transpose map R ⊤ t is a single matrix-vector multiplication with cost O ( bd ) , which is substantially cheaper.

texts.orig: 3.2 Convergence Analysis
texts.text: 3.2 Convergence Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we analyze the optimization performance of Fed-SGM as specified in Algorithm 2. Section 3.2.1 introduces our assumptions on the loss-gradient bounds and Hessian spectrum, and justifies their empirical validity via prior work. Section 3.2.2 then establishes convergence guarantees for Fed-SGM when employing AMSGrad as the GLOBAL_OPT , with the corresponding gradientdescent analysis deferred to Appendix D.
texts.text: In this section, we analyze the optimization performance of Fed-SGM as specified in Algorithm 2. Section 3.2.1 introduces our assumptions on the loss-gradient bounds and Hessian spectrum, and justifies their empirical validity via prior work. Section 3.2.2 then establishes convergence guarantees for Fed-SGM when employing AMSGrad as the GLOBAL_OPT , with the corresponding gradientdescent analysis deferred to Appendix D.

texts.text: Assumption 1 (Bounded Loss Gradients) . There exists a constant G ≥ 0 , such that for every θ ∈ R p and c ∈ [ C ] , ∥∇L c ( θ ) ∥ 2 ≤ G .

We also assume the stochastic noise from mini-batches is sub-Gaussian, which is widely adopted in first-order optimization [25, 48].

texts.text: Assumption 4 (Loss Hessian Eigenspetrum) . Denote { λ i } d i =1 the eigenvectors of the Hessian matrix H L of the average loss function L . Then we assume the absolute intrinsic dimension of H L is bounded, i.e., ∑ d i =1 | λ i | max i λ i ≤ I .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The absolute intrinsic dimension considered here, ∑ d i =1 | λ i | max i λ i , is close to the concept of intrinsic dimension proposed in [26], and the difference is that we consider absolute values of eigenvalues. A growing amount of empirical research suggests that the absolute intrinsic dimension of the Hessian in deep learning can be significantly smaller than the ambient dimension d . Studies by [22, 38, 42] indicate that the eigenspectrum undergoes a sharp decay in magnitude. Additionally, research by [56, 39] demonstrates that a substantial portion of the eigenspectrum is concentrated near zero. Further investigations by [76, 82] reveal that the eigenvalues follow a power-law distribution, suggesting that in such cases, the absolute intrinsic dimension remains a much smaller order than d .

A growing amount of empirical research suggests that the absolute intrinsic dimension of the Hessian in deep learning can be significantly smaller than the ambient dimension d . Studies by [22, 38, 42] indicate that the eigenspectrum undergoes a sharp decay in magnitude. Additionally, research by [56, 39] demonstrates that a substantial portion of the eigenspectrum is concentrated near zero. Further investigations by [76, 82] reveal that the eigenvalues follow a power-law distribution, suggesting that in such cases, the absolute intrinsic dimension remains a much smaller order than d .

texts.orig: Remark 3.2. Regarding the term E AMSGrad s , most of the optimization results for sketching-based algorithms are expectation results [59, 54], while ours is a high-probability optimization bound for the sketching-based FedAvg algorithm. In addition, previous optimization results in expectation either have a linear dependence on the ambient dimension d [59], or get around the dependence by using Top-k components of the gradient vector with rely on heavy-hitter assumptions [54, 80]. As a contrast, our result E AMSGrad s only has a logarithmic dimensional dependence from the need of high probability with no additional mechanisms.
texts.text: Remark 3.2. Regarding the term E AMSGrad s , most of the optimization results for sketching-based algorithms are expectation results [59, 54], while ours is a high-probability optimization bound for the sketching-based FedAvg algorithm. In addition, previous optimization results in expectation either have a linear dependence on the ambient dimension d [59], or get around the dependence by using Top-k components of the gradient vector with rely on heavy-hitter assumptions [54, 80]. As a contrast, our result E AMSGrad s only has a logarithmic dimensional dependence from the need of high probability with no additional mechanisms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 3.3. When there is no clipping activated, i.e., τ ≥ KG , with learning rates η local = O ( √ I √ TK ) , η = O ( 1 √ T I K ) , we can get a the term E AMSGrad s at the order of ˜ O (√ I T ) . When we clip with a constant, i.e., τ = O (1) , by setting η local = O ( 1 √ NTK ) , η = O ( 1 √ T I ) , the order of E AMSGrad s becomes ˜ O ( 1 √ NT + √ I √ TK ) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4 Experiments
texts.text: 4 Experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We conduct empirical evaluations of Fed-SGM on neural network training for both vision and language benchmarks. Across various privacy budgets ϵ p , we compare Fed-SGM using GD and Adam as the global optimizer against their unsketched counterparts-DP-FedAvg [81] for the GD variant and a matched unsketched Adam implementation, and additionally benchmark against DiffSketch [37]. The results are presented after a detailed description of the experimental setup.
texts.text: We conduct empirical evaluations of Fed-SGM on neural network training for both vision and language benchmarks. Across various privacy budgets ϵ p , we compare Fed-SGM using GD and Adam as the global optimizer against their unsketched counterparts-DP-FedAvg [81] for the GD variant and a matched unsketched Adam implementation, and additionally benchmark against DiffSketch [37]. The results are presented after a detailed description of the experimental setup.

texts.label: text

For the language task, we use the SST-2 dataset from the GLUE benchmark [69], which comprises 67349 training samples and 1821 test samples across two sentiment classes. texts.text: handwritten characters. We conduct experiments on ResNet101 [74] with a total of 42M parameter. For the language task, we use the SST-2 dataset from the GLUE benchmark [69], which comprises 67349 training samples and 1821 test samples across two sentiment classes. We finetune a BERT-Base model [14], comprising approximately 100M parameters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Parameter Setting We deploy C = 625 clients in total, sampling N = 4 clients uniformly at random in each communication round. Each selected client executes K = 18 local SGD updates on mini-batches of size 64 , with gradient clipping threshold τ = 1 . Sketching dimension and total rounds are chosen per task: for the vision task, we set b = 4 × 10 5 (approximately 1% compression rate) and run T = 500 communication rounds; for the language task, we use b = 2 × 10 5 (approximately 0 . 2% compression rate) and T = 200 rounds.
texts.text: Parameter Setting We deploy C = 625 clients in total, sampling N = 4 clients uniformly at random in each communication round. Each selected client executes K = 18 local SGD updates on mini-batches of size 64 , with gradient clipping threshold τ = 1 . Sketching dimension and total rounds are chosen per task: for the vision task, we set b = 4 × 10 5 (approximately 1% compression rate) and run T = 500 communication rounds; for the language task, we use b = 2 × 10 5 (approximately 0 . 2% compression rate) and T = 200 rounds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Privacy Level and Noise calculation: For privacy, we fix the parameter δ p = 10 -5 . For both tasks, we consider noise scales σ g ∈ { 0 . 8 , 1 , 2 , 4 } for the unsketched algorithms. By employing the Moments Accountant method [1, 8], we compute the cumulative privacy loss and obtain approximately { 2 . 75 , 1 . 60 , 0 . 42 , 0 . 18 } for the vision task and { 2 . 45 , 1 . 44 , 0 . 35 , 0 . 12 } for the language task, each corresponding to the respective noise scales. For Fed-SGM, at each privacy level, we compute the minimal noise scale σ g by calculating the RDP and minimizing over the Rényi order α numerically [46]. This yields the noise levels approximately { 0 . 0883 , 0 . 1013 , 0 . 1588 , 0 . 2265 } for the vision task and { 0 . 0948 , 0 . 1071 , 0 . 1664 , 0 . 2580 } for the language task. Across all considered privacy budgets in both learning tasks, Fed-SGM attains the same privacy guarantee with strictly lower Gaussian noise variance than the corresponding unsketched algorithm.
texts.text: Privacy Level and Noise calculation: For privacy, we fix the parameter δ p = 10 -5 . For both tasks, we consider noise scales σ g ∈ { 0 . 8 , 1 , 2 , 4 } for the unsketched algorithms. By employing the Moments Accountant method [1, 8], we compute the cumulative privacy loss and obtain approximately { 2 . 75 , 1 . 60 , 0 . 42 , 0 . 18 } for the vision task and { 2 . 45 , 1 . 44 , 0 . 35 , 0 . 12 } for the language task, each corresponding to the respective noise scales. For Fed-SGM, at each privacy level, we compute the minimal noise scale σ g by calculating the RDP and minimizing over the Rényi order α numerically [46]. This yields the noise levels approximately { 0 . 0883 , 0 . 1013 , 0 . 1588 , 0 . 2265 } for the vision task and { 0 . 0948 , 0 . 1071 , 0 . 1664 , 0 . 2580 } for the language task. Across all considered privacy budgets in both learning tasks, Fed-SGM attains the same privacy guarantee with strictly lower Gaussian noise variance than the corresponding unsketched algorithm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experimental Results We report our experimental results in Figure 1 and 2. Additional figures and ablation studies are presented in Appendix F. For each task, Figures 1a and 2a depict the training accuracy of the five algorithms at a fixed privacy budget, whereas Figures 1b and 2b show the corresponding test accuracy. Additionally, Figures 1c and 2c present a trend of the test accuracies of these algorithms under various privacy levels, providing deeper insight into their performance across different privacy constraints. Based on these figures, the following observations can be made:
texts.text: Experimental Results We report our experimental results in Figure 1 and 2. Additional figures and ablation studies are presented in Appendix F. For each task, Figures 1a and 2a depict the training accuracy of the five algorithms at a fixed privacy budget, whereas Figures 1b and 2b show the corresponding test accuracy. Additionally, Figures 1c and 2c present a trend of the test accuracies of these algorithms under various privacy levels, providing deeper insight into their performance across different privacy constraints. Based on these figures, the following observations can be made:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Regardless of the choice of global optimizer, Fed-SGM consistently matches or surpasses its unsketched counterpart, confirming the effectiveness of sketching within a differentially private FL framework. Notably, when Adam is employed, Fed-SGM even outperforms the non-sketching baseline. This improvement arises because Fed-SGM always requires a strictly lower Gaussian noise variance than the unsketched mechanism to attain the same privacy level. The resulting reduction in the amount of injected noise may compensate for any performance degradation typically associated with sketching.
texts.text: Regardless of the choice of global optimizer, Fed-SGM consistently matches or surpasses its unsketched counterpart, confirming the effectiveness of sketching within a differentially private FL framework. Notably, when Adam is employed, Fed-SGM even outperforms the non-sketching baseline. This improvement arises because Fed-SGM always requires a strictly lower Gaussian noise variance than the unsketched mechanism to attain the same privacy level. The resulting reduction in the amount of injected noise may compensate for any performance degradation typically associated with sketching.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Irrespective of whether sketching is employed, the variant using Adam consistently outperforms the ones using gradient descent and the baseline DiffSketch. Notably, even the Adam variant with sketching surpasses the gradient descent variant without sketching, and this trend is maintained across different privacy levels. These results demonstrate the performance enhancement achieved by employing Adam.
texts.text: Irrespective of whether sketching is employed, the variant using Adam consistently outperforms the ones using gradient descent and the baseline DiffSketch. Notably, even the Adam variant with sketching surpasses the gradient descent variant without sketching, and this trend is maintained across different privacy levels. These results demonstrate the performance enhancement achieved by employing Adam.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 4.1. For experiment results in Figure 1 and Figure 2, we use an IID split of the training dataset across all 625 clients. To assess robustness under heterogeneity, we rerun our imageclassification experiments with a non-IID partition using a Dirichlet distribution with concentration parameter 0.05. Table 1 compares test accuracy for models with and without sketching, under both SGD and AMAGrad global optimizers. As expected, overall accuracy decreases under non-IID settings; however, AMSGrad variants still outperforms SGD counterparts, and sketching methods remains competitive with and sometimes outperforms non-sketching ones.

To assess sensitivity to the optimizer choice, we repeated the image-classification experiments with AMSGrad under the same settings as Figure 1. As shown in Table 2, the results with AMSGrad are comparable to those with Adam.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Conclusion
texts.text: 5 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In conclusion, we introduced the Sketched Gaussian Mechanism (SGM), which combines an isometric sketching transform with the classical Gaussian mechanism, and showed via Rényi differential privacy-together with subsampling, post-processing, and composition theorems-that its privacy loss satisfies ϵ = O ( 1 √ bσ 2 g ) without imposing any restrictive upper bound on the sketching di-
texts.text: In conclusion, we introduced the Sketched Gaussian Mechanism (SGM), which combines an isometric sketching transform with the classical Gaussian mechanism, and showed via Rényi differential privacy-together with subsampling, post-processing, and composition theorems-that its privacy loss satisfies ϵ = O ( 1 √ bσ 2 g ) without imposing any restrictive upper bound on the sketching di-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: mension b , thereby demonstrating inherent privacy amplification from sketching. We then embed SGM into a federated learning framework (Fed-SGM) supporting arbitrary server optimizers and prove convergence guarantees that grow only logarithmically in the ambient dimension d and linearly in the empirically small absolute intrinsic Hessian dimension I . Empirical results on vision and language benchmarks confirm that Fed-SGM attains a fixed privacy budget with strictly less noise than unsketched DP-FedAvg, consistently matches or exceeds model accuracy, and benefits further from adaptive optimization. As a direction for future work, we note that our analysis is currently limited to isotropic Gaussian sketching matrices. It therefore remains to establish whether comparable privacy and convergence guarantees hold for more general classes of sketching transforms.
texts.text: mension b , thereby demonstrating inherent privacy amplification from sketching. We then embed SGM into a federated learning framework (Fed-SGM) supporting arbitrary server optimizers and prove convergence guarantees that grow only logarithmically in the ambient dimension d and linearly in the empirically small absolute intrinsic Hessian dimension I . Empirical results on vision and language benchmarks confirm that Fed-SGM attains a fixed privacy budget with strictly less noise than unsketched DP-FedAvg, consistently matches or exceeds model accuracy, and benefits further from adaptive optimization. As a direction for future work, we note that our analysis is currently limited to isotropic Gaussian sketching matrices. It therefore remains to establish whether comparable privacy and convergence guarantees hold for more general classes of sketching transforms.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: NeurIPS Paper Checklist
texts.text: NeurIPS Paper Checklist

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Claims
texts.text: 1. Claims

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper's contributions and scope?
texts.text: Question: Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper's contributions and scope?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: We clearly state our theoretical and experimental claims in our abstract and introduction.
texts.text: Justification: We clearly state our theoretical and experimental claims in our abstract and introduction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The answer NA means that the abstract and introduction do not include the claims made in the paper.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the abstract and introduction do not include the claims made in the paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The abstract and/or introduction should clearly state the claims made, including the contributions made in the paper and important assumptions and limitations. A No or NA answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers.
texts.text: The abstract and/or introduction should clearly state the claims made, including the contributions made in the paper and important assumptions and limitations. A No or NA answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The claims made should match theoretical and experimental results, and reflect how much the results can be expected to generalize to other settings.
texts.text: The claims made should match theoretical and experimental results, and reflect how much the results can be expected to generalize to other settings.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It is fine to include aspirational goals as motivation as long as it is clear that these goals are not attained by the paper.
texts.text: It is fine to include aspirational goals as motivation as long as it is clear that these goals are not attained by the paper.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Limitations
texts.text: 2. Limitations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Does the paper discuss the limitations of the work performed by the authors?
texts.text: Question: Does the paper discuss the limitations of the work performed by the authors?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: We mention the limitation of this research in Section 5.
texts.text: Justification: We mention the limitation of this research in Section 5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The answer NA means that the paper has no limitation while the answer No means that the paper has limitations, but those are not discussed in the paper.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the paper has no limitation while the answer No means that the paper has limitations, but those are not discussed in the paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors are encouraged to create a separate "Limitations" section in their paper.
texts.text: The authors are encouraged to create a separate "Limitations" section in their paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The paper should point out any strong assumptions and how robust the results are to violations of these assumptions (e.g., independence assumptions, noiseless settings, model well-specification, asymptotic approximations only holding locally). The authors should reflect on how these assumptions might be violated in practice and what the implications would be.
texts.text: The paper should point out any strong assumptions and how robust the results are to violations of these assumptions (e.g., independence assumptions, noiseless settings, model well-specification, asymptotic approximations only holding locally). The authors should reflect on how these assumptions might be violated in practice and what the implications would be.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors should reflect on the scope of the claims made, e.g., if the approach was only tested on a few datasets or with a few runs. In general, empirical results often depend on implicit assumptions, which should be articulated.
texts.text: The authors should reflect on the scope of the claims made, e.g., if the approach was only tested on a few datasets or with a few runs. In general, empirical results often depend on implicit assumptions, which should be articulated.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors should reflect on the factors that influence the performance of the approach. For example, a facial recognition algorithm may perform poorly when image resolution is low or images are taken in low lighting. Or a speech-to-text system might not be used reliably to provide closed captions for online lectures because it fails to handle technical jargon.
texts.text: The authors should reflect on the factors that influence the performance of the approach. For example, a facial recognition algorithm may perform poorly when image resolution is low or images are taken in low lighting. Or a speech-to-text system might not be used reliably to provide closed captions for online lectures because it fails to handle technical jargon.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors should discuss the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithms and how they scale with dataset size.
texts.text: The authors should discuss the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithms and how they scale with dataset size.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If applicable, the authors should discuss possible limitations of their approach to address problems of privacy and fairness.
texts.text: If applicable, the authors should discuss possible limitations of their approach to address problems of privacy and fairness.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · While the authors might fear that complete honesty about limitations might be used by reviewers as grounds for rejection, a worse outcome might be that reviewers discover limitations that aren't acknowledged in the paper. The authors should use their best judgment and recognize that individual actions in favor of transparency play an important role in developing norms that preserve the integrity of the community. Reviewers will be specifically instructed to not penalize honesty concerning limitations.
texts.text: While the authors might fear that complete honesty about limitations might be used by reviewers as grounds for rejection, a worse outcome might be that reviewers discover limitations that aren't acknowledged in the paper. The authors should use their best judgment and recognize that individual actions in favor of transparency play an important role in developing norms that preserve the integrity of the community. Reviewers will be specifically instructed to not penalize honesty concerning limitations.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Theory assumptions and proofs
texts.text: 3. Theory assumptions and proofs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: For each theoretical result, does the paper provide the full set of assumptions and a complete (and correct) proof?
texts.text: Question: For each theoretical result, does the paper provide the full set of assumptions and a complete (and correct) proof?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: We provide proofs for all theoretical results in this paper in the appendix, and also supply proof sketches and the associated intuition in the main paper.
texts.text: Justification: We provide proofs for all theoretical results in this paper in the appendix, and also supply proof sketches and the associated intuition in the main paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The answer NA means that the paper does not include theoretical results.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the paper does not include theoretical results.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · All the theorems, formulas, and proofs in the paper should be numbered and crossreferenced.
texts.text: All the theorems, formulas, and proofs in the paper should be numbered and crossreferenced.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · All assumptions should be clearly stated or referenced in the statement of any theorems.
texts.text: All assumptions should be clearly stated or referenced in the statement of any theorems.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The proofs can either appear in the main paper or the supplemental material, but if they appear in the supplemental material, the authors are encouraged to provide a short proof sketch to provide intuition.
texts.text: The proofs can either appear in the main paper or the supplemental material, but if they appear in the supplemental material, the authors are encouraged to provide a short proof sketch to provide intuition.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Inversely, any informal proof provided in the core of the paper should be complemented by formal proofs provided in appendix or supplemental material.
texts.text: Inversely, any informal proof provided in the core of the paper should be complemented by formal proofs provided in appendix or supplemental material.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Theorems and Lemmas that the proof relies upon should be properly referenced.
texts.text: Theorems and Lemmas that the proof relies upon should be properly referenced.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Experimental result reproducibility
texts.text: 4. Experimental result reproducibility

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Does the paper fully disclose all the information needed to reproduce the main experimental results of the paper to the extent that it affects the main claims and/or conclusions of the paper (regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not)?
texts.text: Question: Does the paper fully disclose all the information needed to reproduce the main experimental results of the paper to the extent that it affects the main claims and/or conclusions of the paper (regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not)?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: We disclose all the information needed to reproduce the experimental results in Section 4.
texts.text: Justification: We disclose all the information needed to reproduce the experimental results in Section 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The answer NA means that the paper does not include experiments.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the paper does not include experiments.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If the paper includes experiments, a No answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers: Making the paper reproducible is important, regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not.
texts.text: If the paper includes experiments, a No answer to this question will not be perceived well by the reviewers: Making the paper reproducible is important, regardless of whether the code and data are provided or not.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If the contribution is a dataset and/or model, the authors should describe the steps taken to make their results reproducible or verifiable.
texts.text: If the contribution is a dataset and/or model, the authors should describe the steps taken to make their results reproducible or verifiable.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Depending on the contribution, reproducibility can be accomplished in various ways. For example, if the contribution is a novel architecture, describing the architecture fully might suffice, or if the contribution is a specific model and empirical evaluation, it may be necessary to either make it possible for others to replicate the model with the same dataset, or provide access to the model. In general. releasing code and data is often one good way to accomplish this, but reproducibility can also be provided via detailed instructions for how to replicate the results, access to a hosted model (e.g., in the case of a large language model), releasing of a model checkpoint, or other means that are appropriate to the research performed.
texts.text: Depending on the contribution, reproducibility can be accomplished in various ways. For example, if the contribution is a novel architecture, describing the architecture fully might suffice, or if the contribution is a specific model and empirical evaluation, it may be necessary to either make it possible for others to replicate the model with the same dataset, or provide access to the model. In general. releasing code and data is often one good way to accomplish this, but reproducibility can also be provided via detailed instructions for how to replicate the results, access to a hosted model (e.g., in the case of a large language model), releasing of a model checkpoint, or other means that are appropriate to the research performed.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · While NeurIPS does not require releasing code, the conference does require all submissions to provide some reasonable avenue for reproducibility, which may depend on the nature of the contribution. For example
texts.text: While NeurIPS does not require releasing code, the conference does require all submissions to provide some reasonable avenue for reproducibility, which may depend on the nature of the contribution. For example

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (a) If the contribution is primarily a new algorithm, the paper should make it clear how to reproduce that algorithm.
texts.text: (a) If the contribution is primarily a new algorithm, the paper should make it clear how to reproduce that algorithm.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (b) If the contribution is primarily a new model architecture, the paper should describe the architecture clearly and fully.
texts.text: (b) If the contribution is primarily a new model architecture, the paper should describe the architecture clearly and fully.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (c) If the contribution is a new model (e.g., a large language model), then there should either be a way to access this model for reproducing the results or a way to reproduce the model (e.g., with an open-source dataset or instructions for how to construct the dataset).
texts.text: (c) If the contribution is a new model (e.g., a large language model), then there should either be a way to access this model for reproducing the results or a way to reproduce the model (e.g., with an open-source dataset or instructions for how to construct the dataset).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: (d) We recognize that reproducibility may be tricky in some cases, in which case authors are welcome to describe the particular way they provide for reproducibility. In the case of closed-source models, it may be that access to the model is limited in some way (e.g., to registered users), but it should be possible for other researchers to have some path to reproducing or verifying the results.
texts.text: (d) We recognize that reproducibility may be tricky in some cases, in which case authors are welcome to describe the particular way they provide for reproducibility. In the case of closed-source models, it may be that access to the model is limited in some way (e.g., to registered users), but it should be possible for other researchers to have some path to reproducing or verifying the results.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Open access to data and code
texts.text: 5. Open access to data and code

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Does the paper provide open access to the data and code, with sufficient instructions to faithfully reproduce the main experimental results, as described in supplemental material?
texts.text: Question: Does the paper provide open access to the data and code, with sufficient instructions to faithfully reproduce the main experimental results, as described in supplemental material?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: We provide the link of the code in Appendix F.
texts.text: Justification: We provide the link of the code in Appendix F.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The answer NA means that paper does not include experiments requiring code.
texts.text: The answer NA means that paper does not include experiments requiring code.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Please see the NeurIPS code and data submission guidelines ( https://nips.cc/ public/guides/CodeSubmissionPolicy ) for more details.
texts.text: Please see the NeurIPS code and data submission guidelines ( https://nips.cc/ public/guides/CodeSubmissionPolicy ) for more details.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · While we encourage the release of code and data, we understand that this might not be possible, so 'No' is an acceptable answer. Papers cannot be rejected simply for not including code, unless this is central to the contribution (e.g., for a new open-source benchmark).
texts.text: While we encourage the release of code and data, we understand that this might not be possible, so 'No' is an acceptable answer. Papers cannot be rejected simply for not including code, unless this is central to the contribution (e.g., for a new open-source benchmark).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The instructions should contain the exact command and environment needed to run to reproduce the results. See the NeurIPS code and data submission guidelines ( https: //nips.cc/public/guides/CodeSubmissionPolicy ) for more details.
texts.text: The instructions should contain the exact command and environment needed to run to reproduce the results. See the NeurIPS code and data submission guidelines ( https: //nips.cc/public/guides/CodeSubmissionPolicy ) for more details.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors should provide instructions on data access and preparation, including how to access the raw data, preprocessed data, intermediate data, and generated data, etc.
texts.text: The authors should provide instructions on data access and preparation, including how to access the raw data, preprocessed data, intermediate data, and generated data, etc.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors should provide scripts to reproduce all experimental results for the new proposed method and baselines. If only a subset of experiments are reproducible, they should state which ones are omitted from the script and why.
texts.text: The authors should provide scripts to reproduce all experimental results for the new proposed method and baselines. If only a subset of experiments are reproducible, they should state which ones are omitted from the script and why.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · At submission time, to preserve anonymity, the authors should release anonymized versions (if applicable).
texts.text: At submission time, to preserve anonymity, the authors should release anonymized versions (if applicable).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Providing as much information as possible in supplemental material (appended to the paper) is recommended, but including URLs to data and code is permitted.
texts.text: Providing as much information as possible in supplemental material (appended to the paper) is recommended, but including URLs to data and code is permitted.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Experimental setting/details
texts.text: 6. Experimental setting/details

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Does the paper specify all the training and test details (e.g., data splits, hyperparameters, how they were chosen, type of optimizer, etc.) necessary to understand the results?
texts.text: Question: Does the paper specify all the training and test details (e.g., data splits, hyperparameters, how they were chosen, type of optimizer, etc.) necessary to understand the results?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [Yes]
texts.text: Answer: [Yes]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: We present the training and test details of our experiments in Section 4.
texts.text: Justification: We present the training and test details of our experiments in Section 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The answer NA means that the paper does not include experiments.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the paper does not include experiments.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The experimental setting should be presented in the core of the paper to a level of detail that is necessary to appreciate the results and make sense of them.
texts.text: The experimental setting should be presented in the core of the paper to a level of detail that is necessary to appreciate the results and make sense of them.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The full details can be provided either with the code, in appendix, or as supplemental material.
texts.text: The full details can be provided either with the code, in appendix, or as supplemental material.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. Experiment statistical significance
texts.text: 7. Experiment statistical significance

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Question: Does the paper report error bars suitably and correctly defined or other appropriate information about the statistical significance of the experiments?
texts.text: Question: Does the paper report error bars suitably and correctly defined or other appropriate information about the statistical significance of the experiments?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Answer: [No]
texts.text: Answer: [No]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Justification: Although we do not include error bars in the experiments, we repeat each experiment five times and report the average results.
texts.text: Justification: Although we do not include error bars in the experiments, we repeat each experiment five times and report the average results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Guidelines:
texts.text: Guidelines:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The answer NA means that the paper does not include experiments.
texts.text: The answer NA means that the paper does not include experiments.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The authors should answer "Yes" if the results are accompanied by error bars, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests, at least for the experiments that support the main claims of the paper.
texts.text: The authors should answer "Yes" if the results are accompanied by error bars, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests, at least for the experiments that support the main claims of the paper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The factors of variability that the error bars are capturing should be clearly stated (for example, train/test split, initialization, random drawing of some parameter, or overall run with given experimental conditions).
texts.text: The factors of variability that the error bars are capturing should be clearly stated (for example, train/test split, initialization, random drawing of some parameter, or overall run with given experimental conditions).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The method for calculating the error bars should be explained (closed form formula, call to a library function, bootstrap, etc.)
texts.text: The method for calculating the error bars should be explained (closed form formula, call to a library function, bootstrap, etc.)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The assumptions made should be given (e.g., Normally distributed errors).
texts.text: The assumptions made should be given (e.g., Normally distributed errors).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It should be clear whether the error bar is the standard deviation or the standard error of the mean.
texts.text: It should be clear whether the error bar is the standard deviation or the standard error of the mean.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It is OK to report 1-sigma error bars, but one should state it. The authors should preferably report a 2-sigma error bar than state that they have a 96% CI, if the hypothesis of Normality of errors is not verified.
texts.text: It is OK to report 1-sigma error bars, but one should state it. The authors should preferably report a 2-sigma error bar than state that they have a 96% CI, if the hypothesis of Normality of errors is not verified.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · For asymmetric distributions, the authors should be careful not to show in tables or figures symmetric error bars that would yield results that are out of range (e.g. negative error rates).
texts.text: For asymmetric distributions, the authors should be careful not to show in tables or figures symmetric error bars that would yield results that are out of range (e.g. negative error rates).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · If error bars are reported in tables or plots, The authors should explain in the text how they were calculated and reference the corresponding figures or tables in the text.
texts.text: If error bars are reported in tables or plots, The authors should explain in the text how they were calculated and reference the corresponding figures or tables in the text.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8. Experiments compute resources
texts.text: 8. Experiments compute resources
